**Alicante**[[a]](#cite_note-6) or ***Alacant***[[b]](#cite_note-7) (officially: ***Alacant* / *Alicante***) is a [city](/wiki/City "City") and [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_of_the_Valencian_Community "Municipalities of the Valencian Community") in the [Valencian Community](/wiki/Valencian_Community "Valencian Community"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"). It is the capital of the [province of Alicante](/wiki/Province_of_Alicante "Province of Alicante") and a historic [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") port. With a population of 358,608 as of 2024, it is the [2nd-largest city in the Valencian Community](/wiki/Valencian_Community#Urbanization "Valencian Community") and the [10th-largest in Spain](/wiki/Ranked_lists_of_Spanish_municipalities#By_population "Ranked lists of Spanish municipalities").[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

Together with [Elche](/wiki/Elche "Elche") (*Elx*) and other municipalities, [Alicante forms a conurbation](/wiki/Alicante%E2%80%93Elche_metropolitan_area "Alicante–Elche metropolitan area") of nearly 1 million inhabitants.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name of the city echoes the Arabic name *Laqant* (لَقَنْت‎), *al-Laqant* (اللَّقَنْت‎) or *Al-qant* (القنت‎),[[6]](#cite_note-8) which in turn reflects the Latin *[Lucentum](/wiki/Lucentum "Lucentum")*[[7]](#cite_note-9) and Greek root *Leuké* (or *Leuka*), meaning 'white'.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of Alicante](/wiki/History_of_Alicante "History of Alicante") and [Timeline of Alicante](/wiki/Timeline_of_Alicante "Timeline of Alicante")

The area around Alicante has been inhabited for over 7,000 years. The first tribes of [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") moved gradually from Central Europe between 5000 and 3000 BC. Some of the earlier settlements were made on the slopes of [Mount Benacantil](/wiki/Mount_Benacantil "Mount Benacantil"). By 1000 BC, [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") traders had begun to visit the eastern coast of Spain, establishing small trading ports and introducing the native Iberian tribes to the alphabet, iron, and the pottery wheel. The Carthaginian general [Hamilcar Barca](/wiki/Hamilcar_Barca "Hamilcar Barca") established the fortified settlement of *Akra Leuké* ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): Ἄκρα Λευκή, meaning 'White Mountain' or 'White Point'), in the mid-230s BC, which is generally presumed to have been on the site of modern Alicante.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Tossal-plantagral.jpg/250px-Tossal-plantagral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tossal-plantagral.jpg)

Archeological site of Tossal de Manises, ancient [Iberian](/wiki/Iberians "Iberians")–[Carthaginian](/wiki/Ancient_Carthage "Ancient Carthage")–[Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") city of *Akra Leuké* or *[Lucentum](/wiki/Lucentum "Lucentum")*

Although the Carthaginians conquered much of the land around Alicante, the Romans eventually ruled [Hispania Tarraconensis](/wiki/Hispania_Tarraconensis "Hispania Tarraconensis") for over 700 years. By the 5th century AD, Rome was in decline, and the Roman predecessor town of Alicante, known as *[Lucentum](/wiki/Lucentum "Lucentum")* ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language")), was more or less under the control of the [Visigothic](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") warlord [Theudimer](/wiki/Theodemir_(Visigoth) "Theodemir (Visigoth)") and thereafter under Visigothic rule from 400 to 700 A.D. The Goths did not put up much resistance to the [Arab conquest](/wiki/Umayyad_conquest_of_Hispania "Umayyad conquest of Hispania") of *Medina Laqant* at the beginning of the 8th century. The Moors ruled southern and eastern Spain until the 13th century *[Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista")* (Reconquest). [Alicante was conquered again in 1247](/wiki/Conquest_of_madina_Laqant "Conquest of madina Laqant") for the [Castilian](/wiki/Crown_of_Castile "Crown of Castile") king [Alfonso X](/wiki/Alfonso_X "Alfonso X"), but later was recovered to the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon") in 1296 with King [James II of Aragon](/wiki/James_II_of_Aragon "James II of Aragon"). It gained the status of Royal Village (*Vila Reial*) with representation in the medieval [Valencian Parliament](/wiki/Corts_Valencianes "Corts Valencianes") (*Corts Valencianes*).

After several decades of being the battlefield where the Crown of Castile and the Crown of Aragon clashed, Alicante became a major Mediterranean trading station exporting rice, wine, olive oil, oranges, and wool. The local Jewish community was expelled in 1492,[[8]](#cite_note-10) and later, between 1609 and 1614, [King Felipe III](/wiki/Philip_III_of_Spain "Philip III of Spain") expelled thousands of [Moriscos](/wiki/Morisco "Morisco") who had remained in Valencia after the Reconquista. This act cost the region dearly; with so many skilled artisans and agricultural labourers gone, the feudal nobility found itself sliding into bankruptcy.

Conditions worsened in the early 18th century; after the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"), Alicante went into a long, slow decline, surviving through the 18th and 19th centuries by making shoes and growing agricultural produce such as oranges and almonds, and thanks to its fisheries. The end of the 19th century witnessed a sharp recovery of the local economy with increasing international trade and the growth of the city harbour leading to increased exports of several products (particularly during World War I when Spain was a neutral country).

During the early 20th century, Alicante was a minor capital that took profit from the benefit of Spain's neutrality during World War I, and it provided new opportunities for local industry and agriculture. The [Rif War](/wiki/Rif_War_(1920) "Rif War (1920)") in the 1920s saw numerous *alicantinos* or *alacantins* drafted to fight in the long and bloody campaigns in the former Spanish protectorate (northern Morocco) against the Rif rebels. The political unrest of the late 1920s led to the victory of Republican candidates in local council elections throughout the country, and the abdication of [King Alfonso XIII](/wiki/King_Alfonso_XIII "King Alfonso XIII"). The proclamation of the [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") was much celebrated in the city on 14 April 1931. The [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") broke out on 17 July 1936. Alicante was the last city loyal to the Republican government to be occupied by General Franco's troops on 1 April 1939, and its harbour saw the last Republican government officials fleeing the country. Vicious air bombings were targeted on Alicante during the three years of civil conflict, most notably the [bombing](/wiki/Bombing_of_Alicante "Bombing of Alicante") by the Italian *[Aviazione Legionaria](/wiki/Aviazione_Legionaria "Aviazione Legionaria")* of the [Mercado](/w/index.php?title=Mercado_Central_de_Alicante&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mercado Central de Alicante (page does not exist)") on 25 May 1938 in which more than 300 civilians perished.

The port of Alicante was the site of the heroic episode of the British ship *[SS Stanbrook](/wiki/SS_Stanbrook "SS Stanbrook")* in 1939 at the end of the Spanish Civil War. Her captain [Archibald Dickson](/wiki/Archibald_Dickson_(sea_captain) "Archibald Dickson (sea captain)") decided to rescue thousands of [Spanish Republicans](/wiki/Republican_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Republican faction (Spanish Civil War)") families during the night of 28 March 1939 under the bombing of the Nazis.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

From 1954 onward, many *[pied-noirs](/wiki/Pied-noir "Pied-noir")* settled in the city (as many as 30,000, although other sources decrease the amount tenfold).[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarrigós_MonerrisDaniel_Villa200960-12) Alicante had fostered strong links with [Oran](/wiki/Oran "Oran") in the past, and a notable share of the population of the latter city during the French colonial period had ancestry in the province of Alicante.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarrigós_MonerrisDaniel_Villa200960-12) The immigration process accelerated after the [independence of Algeria in 1962](/wiki/Independence_of_Algeria "Independence of Algeria").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarrigós_MonerrisDaniel_Villa200960-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Playa_de_Alicante.jpg/250px-Playa_de_Alicante.jpg)](/wiki/File:Playa_de_Alicante.jpg)

[El Postiguet beach](/w/index.php?title=El_Postiguet_beach&action=edit&redlink=1 "El Postiguet beach (page does not exist)") in 1957

The late 1950s and early 1960s saw the onset of a lasting transformation of the city by the tourist industry. Large buildings and complexes rose in nearby [Albufereta](/w/index.php?title=Albufereta&action=edit&redlink=1 "Albufereta (page does not exist)"), e.g. [El Barco](/wiki/El_Barco,_Alicante "El Barco, Alicante"), and [Playa de San Juan de Alicante](/wiki/San_Juan_de_Alicante "San Juan de Alicante"), with the benign climate being the biggest draw to attract prospective buyers and tourists who kept the hotels reasonably busy. New construction benefited the whole economy, as the development of the tourism sector also spawned new businesses such as restaurants, bars, and other tourist-oriented enterprises. Also, the old airfield at Rabasa was closed and air traffic moved to the new [El Altet Airport](/wiki/El_Altet_Airport "El Altet Airport"), which made a more convenient and modern facility for charter flights bringing tourists from northern European countries.

When Franco died in 1975, his successor [Juan Carlos I](/wiki/Juan_Carlos_I_of_Spain "Juan Carlos I of Spain") played his part as the living symbol of the transition of Spain to a democratic constitutional monarchy. The governments of regional communities were given constitutional status as *[nationalities](/wiki/Nationalities_and_regions_of_Spain "Nationalities and regions of Spain")*, and their governments were given more autonomy, including that of the Valencian region, the *[Generalitat Valenciana](/wiki/Generalitat_Valenciana "Generalitat Valenciana")*.

The [Port of Alicante](/wiki/Port_of_Alicante "Port of Alicante") has been reinventing itself since the [industrial decline](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") the city suffered in the 1980s (with most mercantile traffic lost to Valencia's harbour). In recent years, the Port Authority has established it as one of the most important ports in Spain for cruises, with 72 calls to port made by cruise ships in 2007 bringing some 80,000 passengers and 30,000 crew to the city each year.[[11]](#cite_note-13) The moves to develop the port for more tourism have been welcomed by the city and its residents, but the latest plans to develop an [industrial estate](/wiki/Industrial_park "Industrial park") in the port have caused great controversy.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Alicante is located in the southeast of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), on the shores of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"). Some orographic features rise over the largely flat terrain where the city is built on including the [Cabo de la Huerta](/w/index.php?title=Cabo_de_la_Huerta&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cabo de la Huerta (page does not exist)"), the [Serra Grossa](/w/index.php?title=Serra_Grossa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Serra Grossa (page does not exist)"), the [Tossal](/w/index.php?title=Tossal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tossal (page does not exist)") and the [Benacantil](/wiki/Benacantil "Benacantil") hills.

Located in an arid territory, Alicante lacks any meaningful permanent water stream.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartínez_Martínez201487-14) There are however several stream beds correspondent to intermittent [*ramblas*](/wiki/Arroyo_(watercourse) "Arroyo (watercourse)").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartínez_Martínez201487-14) There was a swamp area in the northeast of the municipality, *l'Albufereta*, yet it was dried up in 1928.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartínez_Martínez201487-14)

The municipality has two exclaves in the mainland: Monnegre (between the municipalities of [Sant Vicent del Raspeig](/wiki/Sant_Vicent_del_Raspeig_/_San_Vicente_del_Raspeig "Sant Vicent del Raspeig / San Vicente del Raspeig"), [Mutxamel](/wiki/Mutxamel "Mutxamel"), [Busot](/wiki/Busot "Busot") and [Xixona](/wiki/Xixona_/_Jijona "Xixona / Jijona")),[[13]](#cite_note-15) and [Cabeçó d'Or](/w/index.php?title=Cabe%C3%A7%C3%B3_d%27Or&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cabeçó d'Or (page does not exist)"); the latter comprises part of the namesake Cabeçó d'Or mountain (including the summit, 1209 metres above sea level).[[14]](#cite_note-16) The small island of [Tabarca](/wiki/Tabarca "Tabarca"), 8 [nautical miles](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") to the south of the city, also belongs to the municipality.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

The foot of the main staircase of the City Hall Building (*[ayuntamiento](/wiki/Ayuntamiento "Ayuntamiento")* or *ajuntament*; also known as *casa consistorial*) is the zero point (*cota cero*), used as the point of reference for measuring the height above or below sea level of any point in Spain, due to the marginal tidal variations of the Mediterranean sea at Alicante.[[16]](#cite_note-18)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Alicante has mild winter temperatures, hot and sultry summers, and little rain, concentrated in equinoctial periods. Like the rest of the [Province of Alicante](/wiki/Province_of_Alicante "Province of Alicante") itself, which has a range of dry climate types, the city has a [hot semi-arid climate](/wiki/Hot_semi-arid_climate "Hot semi-arid climate") (*BSh*) according to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification").[[17]](#cite_note-19) It is one of the driest cities in Europe.[[18]](#cite_note-20) Daily variations in temperature are generally small because of the stabilising influence of the sea, although occasional periods of westerly wind can produce temperature changes of 15 °C (27 °F) or more. Seasonal temperature variations are also relatively small, meaning that winters are mild and summers are hot. During the summer, due to the evaporation of warm Mediterranean waters, air [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity") levels are high, making the day and night stuffy for much of the season. These high humidity levels increase the [heat index](/wiki/Heat_index "Heat index").

The average rainfall is 284.5 mm (11.2 in) per year. The [cold drop](/wiki/Cold_drop "Cold drop") means that September and October are the wettest months. Rarely, the rainfall can be torrential, reaching over 100 mm (4 in) in a 24-hour period, leading to severe flooding. Because of this irregularity, only 35 rainy days are observed on average per year, and the annual number of sunshine hours is more than 3,000.

The record maximum temperature of 42.0 °C (107.6 °F) was observed on 13 August 2022.[[19]](#cite_note-21) The record minimum temperature of −4.6 °C (23.7 °F) was recorded on 12 February 1956.[[20]](#cite_note-22) The worst flooding in the city's modern history occurred on 30 September 1997 when 270.2 mm (10.6 in) of rain fell within six hours.[[21]](#cite_note-Desalination2001-23) Temperatures below 2 °C (36 °F) are very rare; the last recorded snowfall occurred in 1926.[[22]](#cite_note-24) Alicante enjoys one of the sunniest and warmest winter daytime temperatures in [mainland Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe").[[23]](#cite_note-25) Alicante also recorded the highest temperature ever recorded in [peninsular Spain](/wiki/Peninsular_Spain "Peninsular Spain") and one of the highest in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") for a month of January, which was 29.8 °C (85.6 °F).[[24]](#cite_note-26)[[25]](#cite_note-27)

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Administration")]

See also: [List of mayors of Alicante](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Alicante "List of mayors of Alicante")

[Luis Barcala](/wiki/Luis_Barcala "Luis Barcala") of the [People's Party](/wiki/People%27s_Party_(Spain) "People's Party (Spain)") has been the mayor of Alicante since 19 April 2018. He became mayor after the resignation of Gabriel Echávarri, when the councillor Nerea Belmonte defected from Guanyar Alacant and refused to support the Socialist Party replacement candidate Eva Montesinos.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Sal%C3%B3n_de_Plenos_del_Ayuntamiento_de_Alicante.JPG/250px-Sal%C3%B3n_de_Plenos_del_Ayuntamiento_de_Alicante.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sal%C3%B3n_de_Plenos_del_Ayuntamiento_de_Alicante.JPG)

Plenary hall where the municipal council meets.

Gabriel Echávarri of the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Spanish_Socialist_Workers%27_Party "Spanish Socialist Workers' Party") (PSOE) was the mayor of the city from 13 June 2015 until April 2018, following the [municipal elections](/wiki/2015_Spanish_local_elections "2015 Spanish local elections") on 24 May 2015. He was supported by the votes from his group (6), plus those from leftist parties [Guanyar Alacant](/w/index.php?title=Guanyar_Alacant&action=edit&redlink=1 "Guanyar Alacant (page does not exist)") (6) and [Compromís](/wiki/Comprom%C3%ADs "Compromís") (3), as well as from the centre-right party [Ciudadanos](/wiki/Ciudadanos "Ciudadanos") (6). The People's Party (*Partido Popular*, PP), with only 8 elected seats, lost the majority. On April he resigned due to various judicial issues and was temporarily substituted by the councillor Eva Montesinos.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

In the previous [municipal elections](/wiki/2011_Spanish_local_elections "2011 Spanish local elections") of May 2011, [Sonia Castedo](/wiki/Sonia_Castedo "Sonia Castedo") of People's Party won the elections with an [absolute majority](/wiki/Majority "Majority"), but resigned in December 2014 due to her involvement in several corruption scandals, at present being under investigation. Her fellow party member [Miguel Valor](/w/index.php?title=Miguel_Valor&action=edit&redlink=1 "Miguel Valor (page does not exist)") went on to become mayor up until Echávarri's election.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Port_of_Alicante_7.jpg/250px-Port_of_Alicante_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Port_of_Alicante_7.jpg)

Port of Alicante

Until the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), Alicante was one of the fastest-growing cities in Spain. The boom depended partly on tourism directed to the beaches of the [Costa Blanca](/wiki/Costa_Blanca "Costa Blanca") and particularly on the second residence-construction boom which started in the 1960s and revived again by the late 1990s. [Services](/wiki/Tertiary_sector "Tertiary sector") and public administration also play a major role in the city's economy. The construction boom has raised many environmental concerns and both the local autonomous government and city council are under scrutiny by the European Union. The construction surge was the subject of hot debates among politicians and citizens alike. The latest of many public battles concerns the plans of the Port Authority of Alicante to construct an industrial estate on reclaimed land in front of the city's coastal strip, in breach of local, national, and European regulations. (See [Port of Alicante](/wiki/Port_of_Alicante "Port of Alicante") for details).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Vista_aerea_ciudad_luz.jpg/250px-Vista_aerea_ciudad_luz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vista_aerea_ciudad_luz.jpg)

Ciudad de la Luz or *Ciutat de la Llum*

The city serves as the headquarters of the [European Union Intellectual Property Office](/wiki/European_Union_Intellectual_Property_Office "European Union Intellectual Property Office") and a sizeable population of European public workers live there.

The campus of the [University of Alicante](/wiki/University_of_Alicante "University of Alicante") (*Universitat d'Alacant* or *Universidad de Alicante*) lies in [Sant Vicent del Raspeig](/wiki/Sant_Vicent_del_Raspeig_/_San_Vicente_del_Raspeig "Sant Vicent del Raspeig / San Vicente del Raspeig"), bordering the city of Alicante to the north. More than 25,000 students attend the university.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

Between 2005 and 2012 [Ciudad de la Luz](/wiki/Ciudad_de_la_Luz "Ciudad de la Luz") (*Ciutat de la Llum*), one of the largest film studios in Europe, had its base in Alicante. The studio shot Spanish and international movies such as [*Asterix at the Olympic Games*](/wiki/Asterix_at_the_Olympic_Games_(film) "Asterix at the Olympic Games (film)") by [Frédéric Forestier](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Forestier "Frédéric Forestier") and [Thomas Langmann](/wiki/Thomas_Langmann "Thomas Langmann"), and [*Manolete*](/wiki/Manolete_(film) "Manolete (film)") by [Menno Meyjes](/wiki/Menno_Meyjes "Menno Meyjes"). It was shut down in 2012 for violating European competition law.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transport")]

[Alicante Airport](/wiki/Alicante_Airport "Alicante Airport") outranks the [Valencia Airport](/wiki/Valencia_Airport "Valencia Airport"), being the busiest airport in the [Valencian Community](/wiki/Valencian_Community "Valencian Community"), and among the busiest airports in Spain after [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid_Airport "Madrid Airport"), [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona_Airport "Barcelona Airport"), [Palma de Mallorca](/wiki/Palma_de_Mallorca_Airport "Palma de Mallorca Airport") and [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga_Airport "Málaga Airport"). It is connected with Madrid and Barcelona by frequent [Iberia](/wiki/Iberia_Airlines "Iberia Airlines") and [Vueling](/wiki/Vueling_Airlines "Vueling Airlines") flights, and with many Western European cities through carriers such as [Ryanair](/wiki/Ryanair "Ryanair"), [EasyJet](/wiki/EasyJet "EasyJet") and [Jet2](/wiki/Jet2 "Jet2"). There are also regular flights to [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria").

[Alicante railway station](/wiki/Alicante_railway_station "Alicante railway station") is used by [Cercanías Murcia/Alicante](/wiki/Cercan%C3%ADas_Murcia/Alicante "Cercanías Murcia/Alicante") [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") services linking Alicante with suburbs and [Murcia](/wiki/Murcia "Murcia").[[38]](#cite_note-40) Long-range [Renfe](/wiki/Renfe "Renfe") trains run frequently to [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), and [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia").[[39]](#cite_note-41) In 2013, the [Madrid–Levante high-speed rail network](/wiki/Madrid%E2%80%93Levante_high-speed_rail_network "Madrid–Levante high-speed rail network") was extended to Alicante station, allowing [AVE](/wiki/AVE "AVE") [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") services to link to Madrid via [Villena AV](/wiki/Villena_AV_railway_station "Villena AV railway station"), [Albacete-Los Llanos](/wiki/Albacete-Los_Llanos_railway_station "Albacete-Los Llanos railway station") and [Cuenca-Fernando Zóbel](/wiki/Cuenca%E2%80%93Fernando_Z%C3%B3bel_railway_station "Cuenca–Fernando Zóbel railway station").

[Alicante Metropolitan-Tram](/wiki/Alicante_Tram "Alicante Tram") connects different parts within the city, its metropolitan area and with outlying settlements along [Costa Blanca](/wiki/Costa_Blanca "Costa Blanca") as well. As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit), electric [tram-trains](/wiki/Tram-train "Tram-train") run up to [Benidorm](/wiki/Benidorm "Benidorm"), and diesel trains go further to [Dénia](/wiki/D%C3%A9nia "Dénia").[[40]](#cite_note-42)

The city has regular [ferry](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry") services to Algeria.[[41]](#cite_note-43) The city is strongly fortified, with a spacious harbour.

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Main sights")]

Amongst the most notable features of the city are the [Castle of Santa Bárbara](/wiki/Castle_of_Santa_B%C3%A1rbara "Castle of Santa Bárbara") and the [port of Alicante](/wiki/Port_of_Alicante "Port of Alicante"). The latter was the subject of bitter controversy in 2006–2007 as residents battled, successfully, to keep it from being changed into an industrial estate.

The Santa Bárbara castle is situated on [Mount Benacantil](/wiki/Mount_Benacantil "Mount Benacantil"), overlooking the city. The tower (*La Torreta*) at the top, is the oldest part of the castle, while part of the lowest zone and the walls were constructed later in the 18th century.

The promenade *Explanada de España*, lined by palm trees, is paved with 6.5 million marble floor tiles creating a wavy form. The Promenade extends from the Port of Alicante to the Gran Vía and ends at the famous statue by Mark Hersch. For the people of Alicante, the promenade is the meeting place for the traditional Spanish *paseo*, or stroll along the waterfront in the evenings, and a venue for outdoor musical concerts. At the end of the promenade is a monument by the artist Bañuls of the 19th century.

*Barrio de la Santa Cruz* is a colourful quarter of the old city, situated southwest of Santa Bárbara castle. Its small houses climb up the hill leading to the walls and the castle, through narrow streets decorated with flags and tubs of flowers.

*L'Ereta Park* is situated on the foothills of Mount Benacantil. It runs from the Santa Bárbara castle down to the old part of Alicante and consists of several levels, routes, decks, and rest stops which offer a panoramic view overlooking the city.

*El Palmeral Park* is one of the favourite parks of Alicante's citizens. It includes walking trails, children's playgrounds, ponds and brooks, picnic tables, and an auditorium for concerts.

Just a few kilometres from Alicante, on the Mediterranean Sea, lies [Tabarca](/wiki/Tabarca "Tabarca") island. What was once a haven for Barbary pirates is now a tourist attraction.

Other sights include:

* [Basilica of Santa María](/wiki/Basilica_of_Santa_Maria,_Alicante "Basilica of Santa Maria, Alicante") (14th–16th centuries), built-in [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") style over the former main mosque. Other features include the high altar, in Rococo style, and the portal, in [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") style, both from the 18th century.
* [Co-cathedral of St. Nicholas of Bari](/wiki/San_Nicolas_Cathedral,_Alicante "San Nicolas Cathedral, Alicante") (15th–18th centuries), also built over a mosque. It is the main church of Alicante and the bishop's seat.
* Monastery of *Santa Faz* (15th century), located 5 kilometres (3 miles) outside the city, in Baroque style.
* Defence towers of the *Huerta de Alicante* (15th–18th centuries), built to defend against the Barbary pirates. Today some 20 towers are still extant.
* Baroque *Casa de La Asegurada* (1685), the most ancient civil building in the city. (c. XVII). Today it is home to the [Museum of Contemporary Art of Alicante](/wiki/Museum_of_Contemporary_Art_of_Alicante "Museum of Contemporary Art of Alicante").
* *Casa consistorial de Alicante* (18th century), also in Baroque style.
* Convent of the *Canónigas de San Agustín* (18th century).
* Gravina Palace (1748–1808), nowadays hosting [Gravina Museum of Fine Arts](/wiki/Gravina_Museum_of_Fine_Arts "Gravina Museum of Fine Arts").
* Castle of San Fernando.

There are a dozen museums in Alicante. On exhibition at the [Archaeological Museum of Alicante](/wiki/Archaeological_Museum_of_Alicante "Archaeological Museum of Alicante") (MARQ) are local artifacts dating from 100,000 years ago until the early 20th century. The collection is divided into different rooms representing three divisions of archaeological methodology: ground, urban and underwater archaeology, with [dioramas](/wiki/Dioramas "Dioramas"), audiovisual and interactive zones. The archaeological museum won the [European Museum of the Year Award](/wiki/European_Museum_of_the_Year_Award "European Museum of the Year Award") in 2004. [Gravina Museum of Fine Arts](/wiki/Gravina_Museum_of_Fine_Arts "Gravina Museum of Fine Arts") presents several paintings and sculptures from the 16th century to the 19th century. [Asegurada Museum of Contemporary Art](/wiki/Asegurada_Museum_of_Contemporary_Art "Asegurada Museum of Contemporary Art") houses a major collection of twentieth-century art, composed mainly of works donated by [Eusebio Sempere](/wiki/Eusebio_Sempere "Eusebio Sempere").

## Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Festivals")]

The most important festival, the *[Bonfires of Saint John](/wiki/Bonfires_of_Saint_John "Bonfires of Saint John")* (*Hogueras de San Juan* / *Fogueres de Sant Joan*), takes place during the summer [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice"). This is followed a week later by five nights of firework and pyrotechnic contests between companies on the urban beach *Playa del Postiguet*. Another well-known festival is *[Moors and Christians](/wiki/Moros_i_Cristians "Moros i Cristians")* (*Moros y Cristianos*) in Altozano or *San Blas* district. Overall, the city boasts a year-round nightlife for the enjoyment of tourists, residents, and a large student population of the [University of Alicante](/wiki/University_of_Alicante "University of Alicante"). The nightlife social scene tends to shift to nearby Playa de San Juan during the summer months.

Every summer in Alicante, a two-month-long programme of music, theatre and dance is staged in the Paseo del Puerto.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alicante&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sport")]

For the 2023/24 season Alicante has two football clubs in the top four levels of Spanish football; [Hércules CF](/wiki/H%C3%A9rcules_CF "Hércules CF") and [CF Intercity](/wiki/CF_Intercity "CF Intercity"). For the 2023/24 season Hércules compete in [Segunda Federación](/wiki/Segunda_Federaci%C3%B3n "Segunda Federación"), the 4th level and are well known as they played in [La Liga (the Spanish Premier Division)](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") during the 1996/1997 season and again in 2010/2011. They have had many famous players such as [David Trezeguet](/wiki/David_Trezeguet "David Trezeguet"), [Royston Drenthe](/wiki/Royston_Drenthe "Royston Drenthe") and [Nelson Valdez](/wiki/Nelson_Valdez "Nelson Valdez"). Hércules are also known for their victory over Barcelona in 1997 which led to Real Madrid winning the league. Home games are played at the 30,000-capacity [José Rico Pérez Stadium](/wiki/Estadio_Jos%C3%A9_Rico_P%C3%A9rez "Estadio José Rico Pérez").

The city's other club, [Alicante CF](/wiki/Alicante_CF "Alicante CF"), who played in the [Third Division](/wiki/Tercera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Tercera División"), was dissolved in 2014 due to economic problems. They were [replaced](/wiki/Phoenix_club_(sports) "Phoenix club (sports)") the same year by newly formed club [CFI Alicante](/wiki/CFI_Alicante "CFI Alicante").

Basketball club (HLA Alicante) [Lucentum Alicante](/wiki/CB_Lucentum_Alicante "CB Lucentum Alicante") participates in the [Spanish basketball league](/wiki/Liga_ACB "Liga ACB"). It plays in the [Centro de Tecnificación de Alicante](/wiki/Centro_de_Tecnificaci%C3%B3n_de_Alicante "Centro de Tecnificación de Alicante").

Alicante serves as headquarters and the starting point of the [Volvo Ocean Race](/wiki/Volvo_Ocean_Race "Volvo Ocean Race"), a yacht race around the world. The [latest race](/wiki/2023_The_Ocean_Race "2023 The Ocean Race") sailed in January 2023.
